topic: Why Salt Makes Even Sweet Things Taste Better

packaging.title: Why a Pinch of Salt Makes Caramel Taste Sweeter
altTitles: The Salt Trick Hiding in Every Dessert
Why Salt Belongs in Your Chocolate Chip Cookies
packaging.description: A food science look at why a tiny bit of salt makes sweet foods like caramel, cookies, and watermelon taste even sweeter, and how salt quietly silences bitterness on your tongue.

thumbnail.visualIdea: A glossy caramel square with a few coarse salt flakes landing on top, single hero object on dark background

packaging.hookPromise: The next time you make caramel, leaving out one pinch of salt is the difference between good and unforgettable — and it has nothing to do with the salt tasting salty.
openLoops: How can salt make something taste sweeter when salt isn't sweet at all?
Why does a salted watermelon taste juicier and sweeter than a plain slice?
What is salt secretly hiding on your tongue that you never noticed was there?
packaging.payoff: Salt doesn't boost sweetness directly — it suppresses your bitter receptors, removing a hidden bitterness that was quietly masking the sugar all along, so the sweetness you tasted was there the whole time.
packaging.whyItWorks: Everyone has salted caramel or watermelon and wondered why it works, so the curiosity gap is universal and the named dessert makes it instantly clickable.
